did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a machine learning classifier (specifically a feature-based regression classifier with PCA and logistic regression) to binary classify suspicious pigmented lesions from wide-field images, achieving a sensitivity of 100% for confirmed suspicious lesions and 83.2% for unconfirmed suspicious lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the acquisition of a new clinical database of 1759 pigmented lesions from 133 patients, the development and optimization of a machine learning classifier (feature-based regression with PCA and logistic regression), and the reporting of specific performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) on a test set. It is not a review or summary of prior literature.

title: Computer-aided classification of suspicious pigmented lesions using wide-field images
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study developed a computer-aided classification system to distinguish suspicious from non-suspicious pigmented lesions using wide-field images acquired by a consumer-grade camera. The researchers created a new clinical database comprising 1759 pigmented lesions from 133 high-risk patients, which were evaluated by a board-certified dermatologist. A feature-based regression classifier utilizing PCA and logistic regression was optimized to assign a suspiciousness score to each lesion. The system was evaluated on a test set, achieving a sensitivity of 100% for lesions confirmed as suspicious by dermoscopy and 83.2% for those not confirmed, with an overall accuracy of 75.9%. The results demonstrate that wide-field photography combined with this classification approach can effectively support primary care skin screenings by identifying lesions requiring further examination.

lesion_types_studied.details: Binary classification target. SPL_A: Suspicious clinical features detected by naked eye and confirmed by dermoscopy (n=55 total, 9 biopsied). SPL_B: Suspicious clinical features by naked eye but not confirmed by dermoscopy (n=172 in test set). The study distinguishes SPLs from non-SPLs rather than specific histopathological diagnoses.
Binary classification target. Lesions without suspicious clinical features detected by naked eye examination.
Found in 3 of the 9 biopsied SPL_A lesions. Mentioned as the primary disease context for early identification, but not a direct classification label in the machine learning model.
Found in 2 of the 9 biopsied SPL_A lesions.
Found in 2 of the 9 biopsied SPL_A lesions.
Found in 2 of the 9 biopsied SPL_A lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of pigmented lesions into 'suspicious' (SPL) or 'non-suspicious' (non-SPL) categories, with sub-classifications for SPL_A (confirmed suspicious) and SPL_B (unconfirmed suspicious).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'computer-aided classification system' and a 'machine learning classifier' optimized to 'binary classify each lesion using a suspiciousness score'. The model uses logistic regression to predict discrete diagnostic categories (suspicious vs. non-suspicious) based on extracted features. Although a 'suspiciousness score' is generated, it is used as an intermediate value for binary decision-making (thresholding at 0.46) rather than as a continuous regression target. The primary task is distinguishing between predefined classes.

datasets_used.name: New Clinical Database of Wide-Field Images
datasets_used.description: A newly acquired clinical database comprising wide-field images of major body parts (back, torso, legs, arms, other) taken with a consumer-grade Olympus E-420 camera under natural illumination from 133 high-risk patients. Lesions were manually cropped and analyzed. The study distinguishes this from standard dermoscopy datasets by its use of wide-field photography to simulate primary care screening conditions.
classes_used: Suspicious Pigmented Lesion Class A (SPL_A): Suspicious clinical features detected by naked eye and confirmed by dermoscopy/histopathology.
Suspicious Pigmented Lesion Class B (SPL_B): Suspicious clinical features detected by naked eye but not confirmed by dermoscopy.
Non-Suspicious Pigmented Lesion (non-SPL): Lesions without suspicious clinical features.

use.training: The dataset was used to develop and optimize the feature-based regression classifier (PCA and logistic regression). The optimization process used cross-validation (leave-one-out) on a subset of the data (1141 non-SPLs and 46 SPLAs mentioned in validation context, though total training split is not explicitly defined as a separate static set distinct from the optimization loop).
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter optimization and threshold selection via leave-one-out cross-validation. The validation set consisted of 1141 non-SPLs and 46 SPLAs.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final classifier performance. The test set contained 572 lesions (181 SPLs [9 SPL_A, 172 SPL_B] and 391 non-SPLs).
use.other: The dataset was created specifically for this study to address the lack of wide-field image data for computer-aided classification in primary care settings.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Hyper-parameter optimization for the machine learning classifier.
Generation of the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and calculation of the Area Under the Curve (AUC) with confidence intervals.
Hyper-parameter optimization (exploring ranges for k-best reduction, PCA components, and regularization strength).
Feature selection to reduce dimensionality by selecting the k highest scoring features.
Dimensionality reduction of the extracted features.
Uncertainty estimation for the Area Under the Curve (AUC) metric.
Evaluation of classifier performance and determination of the suspiciousness score threshold.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for the comparison of classifier performance. It reported performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) and 95% confidence intervals for the AUC, but no statistical hypothesis tests were described.

New Clinical Database of Wide-Field Imag.notes: The classifier was optimized using 10-fold cross-validation on a training set (1187 lesions: 46 SPL_A, 1141 non-SPL). The final model was evaluated on a separate test set (572 lesions: 181 SPL [9 SPL_A, 172 SPL_B], 391 non-SPL). The AUC was calculated using leave-one-out cross-validation on the training set. There was a statistically significant difference (p < 0.001) between non-SPL and SPL suspiciousness scores.

architecture_used.main_model: Feature-based regression classifier using Logistic Regression
architecture_used.input_size: Lesion images extracted from wide-field photographs (resolution varies, e.g., 3648 × 2736 pixels for a 20.4 × 15.3 cm image); input to classifier is a feature vector of 399 extracted features

architecture_details.feature_extraction: 399 features extracted based on ABCD criteria (Asymmetry: 91, Border: 192, Color: 48, Texture: 66, Pixel size: 1, Lesion area: 1). Features include variations of color (HSV space), border irregularity, asymmetry, and texture (co-occurrence matrices).
architecture_details.feature_selection: Univariate feature selection (ANOVA) to select top k features, followed by Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction.
architecture_details.classifier_type: L2 penalized Logistic Regression with class balancing (implemented in Scikit-learn)
architecture_details.optimization_method: Random search with 105 iterations, optimizing for AUC using 10-fold cross-validation

training_details.validation_method: 10-fold cross-validation for hyperparameter optimization; Leave-one-out cross-validation for ROC curve generation

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Binary classification: score < 0.46 is non-suspicious, score >= 0.46 is suspicious

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: L2 penalized logistic regression with class balancing
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RAW images to TIFF format using camera-specific software (OLYMPUS Viewer 3)
Manual cropping of single lesions with diameter > 3 mm from original wide-field images
Illumination correction using Otsu's method for automatic thresholding of grayscale images
Lesion border segmentation using an automatic algorithm by Celebi et al. to separate lesion from surrounding skin
Blob-detection approach to select the centered blob as the lesion
Morphological opening operation with a disk-shaped structuring element spanning one-fifth of the total lesion image size to remove foreground objects
Application of Gaussian blur to reduce high-frequency components of the background while maintaining non-uniform illumination information
Shading attenuation method by Cavalcanti et al. to correct inhomogeneous illumination artifacts
Subtraction of the lightness value in HSV space of the opened image after Gaussian blurring from the original color image pixel by pixel
Standardization of feature data by scaling each feature distribution to have zero mean and unit mean before input into the model
conclusions: Wide-field photography combined with computer-aided classification systems can effectively distinguish suspicious from non-suspicious pigmented lesions.
The proposed system achieved a sensitivity of 100% for suspicious pigmented lesions confirmed by dermoscopy (SPL_A) and 83.2% for those not confirmed (SPL_B), with an overall accuracy of 75.9%.
The system's suspiciousness score is aligned with common macro-screening (naked eye) practices, potentially aiding in the assessment of lesion severity.
This approach may be effective for supporting skin screenings at a population level, particularly in primary care settings where wide-field images are acquired under natural illumination.

The study demonstrates the utility of using consumer-grade cameras and wide-field images for computer-aided diagnosis, overcoming limitations of traditional systems that rely on pre-selected, controlled dermoscopy images.

Limitations include misclassifications due to segmentation errors (e.g., lesions with excess hair or depigmented halos), suggesting that future improvements could involve incorporating more surrounding non-lesion information.
important_remarks: The study population consists exclusively of high-risk patients (133 patients with family or personal history of melanoma), limiting generalizability to the general primary care population.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with only 9 SPL_A lesions (confirmed suspicious) versus 1141 non-SPL lesions in the training set, potentially biasing the classifier.

SPL_A lesions were confirmed by dermoscopy/histopathology, while SPL_B lesions were suspicious by naked eye but not confirmed by dermoscopy; the classifier distinguishes clinical suspicion rather than histopathological malignancy directly.
Only 9 of the 55 SPL_A lesions were biopsied, providing limited histopathological ground truth for the 'confirmed suspicious' class.

The system relies on manual cropping of lesions from wide-field images, which limits scalability and introduces potential segmentation errors (e.g., lesions with excess hair or depigmented halos were misclassified).
No external validation was performed; the model was trained and tested on data from the same cohort of high-risk patients.

The classifier uses a suspiciousness score aligned with macro-screening (naked eye) practices rather than traditional melanoma vs. non-melanoma classification, which may have different clinical utility and interpretation.
The study acknowledges that patient information (history, age, sex) was not included due to availability, suggesting potential for improved performance with multimodal input.
The test set included 100% of SPL_B lesions but only approx. 25% of SPL_A and non-SPL lesions, indicating a non-random split that may affect performance estimation.
The system was designed to work with consumer-grade cameras under natural illumination, enhancing real-world applicability but introducing variability in lighting and image quality.
